APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This paper falls squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues: it introduces a new collaborative dialogue task and a substantial 11K human-human dataset, proposes a novel model (DynoNet) combining graph-structured KB and dialogue history, and uses standard automatic and human evaluation metrics (fluency, correctness, cooperation, human-likeness, task success). The methods, baselines (rule-based and neural), and evaluations are appropriate for the community, and the problem formulation and positioning align well with research on task-oriented and conversational agents.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper introduces a genuinely new symmetric collaborative dialogue setting and supports it with a substantial 11K human-human dialogue dataset, which appears to be a novel problem formulation compared to typical one-sided KB-grounded dialogue. The proposed DynoNet model—dynamic knowledge graph embeddings that update node/entity embeddings via message passing and combine structured KB with unstructured dialogue through entity abstraction—represents a non-trivial technical extension beyond standard encoder-decoder or static-KB approaches. The authors explicitly differentiate their contributions (new setting, dataset, and modeling choices) from prior work and provide empirical/human evaluations showing benefits, so the contribution is notably novel and would interest experts.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares its DynoNet model against both rule-based and neural baselines on the collected 11K-dialogue dataset using automatic and human evaluation metrics (including task success), and reports ablation studies (e.g., message-passing iterations and entity abstraction). Related work coverage is described as thorough, indicating relevant approaches are cited. However, the summary does not specify which recent neural baselines were used or whether any prior methods ever matched or outperformed the proposed model, so there are minor omissions in baseline specification and explicit acknowledgment of comparable prior performance.

IMPACT.justification: The paper addresses an important and underexplored problem—symmetric collaborative dialogue with private knowledge bases—and provides a substantial empirical resource (an 11K human-human dialogue dataset) that is likely to be valuable to the community. Its DynoNet model, which uses dynamic knowledge-graph embeddings, message passing, and entity abstraction to fuse structured KBs with unstructured dialogue, demonstrably improves task success and human-likeness in both automatic and human evaluations. This combination of a new setting, a sizable dataset, and a principled modeling approach is likely to change how researchers approach multi-agent collaborative dialogue and to open meaningful follow-on work, though it is not necessarily transformative across all of NLP.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper runs comprehensive automatic and human evaluations (loss, task success, fluency/correctness/cooperation/human-likeness) on a large collected dataset (11K dialogues) and compares against rule-based and neural baselines, supporting the main claims. Appropriate ablation/sensitivity analyses are reported (varying message-passing iterations K, removing entity abstraction, and a sum variant), and related work coverage is described as thorough. Limitations and failure modes are discussed (performance drops with reduced K and without entity abstraction). One minor reproducibility gap remains: hyperparameters are reported but code availability is not mentioned, so full replication may be harder.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by empirical evidence: a large 11K-dialogue dataset, a concrete DynoNet model with message-passing and entity abstraction, and automatic plus human evaluations reporting improved task success and human-likeness. There are no complex mathematical proofs to verify and the model description (dynamic KG embeddings, message passing, copy mechanism) is coherent. Experimental methodology seems generally sound (rule-based and neural baselines, ablations on K and entity abstraction, human evaluations), but the summary omits details about baseline implementations and statistical testing, which are minor gaps. Overall, the conclusions are justified by the reported results though fuller methodological details would strengthen confidence.

CLARITY.justification: The paper appears well-organized with a clear methodology (DynoNet) followed by comprehensive experiments (automatic and human evaluations) and a concise conclusion; main contributions are stated early and writing quality is noted as good. Related work coverage is thorough and hyperparameters are reported, which supports clarity and reproducibility. A minor presentation shortcoming is that the summary does not mention code availability or details about figures/tables, so small improvements in supplemental clarity likely prevent a top score.

main_claims: Introduce a symmetric collaborative dialogue setting where two agents, each with a private knowledge base, must communicate to identify a shared item.
Collected a large dataset of 11K human-human dialogues for this setting, exhibiting diverse lexical, semantic, and strategic phenomena.

Propose a neural model with dynamic knowledge graph embeddings (DynoNet) that updates node/entity embeddings via message passing and uses entity abstraction to combine structured KB and unstructured dialogue.
Automatic and human evaluations show the proposed model is more effective at achieving the task goal and more human-like than baseline neural and rule-based models.
_summary.methodology: A recurrent encoder-decoder model that maintains an evolving knowledge graph over KB and dialogue history; node embeddings are updated by message passing and entity abstraction, and generation uses attention over node embeddings with a copy mechanism to produce utterances.
datasets: Collected symmetric collaborative dialogue dataset (11K human-human dialogues)
metrics: Human evaluations: fluency, correctness, cooperation, human-likeness
Task success (both agents correctly select the shared item)
limitations: Performance degrades when the number of message passing iterations K is reduced (K=2 -> K=0 increases loss).
Removing entity abstraction degrades performance compared to the full model.
Using sum (in a component) slightly hurts performance.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - dialogue systems / collaborative task-oriented dialogue
